element_choice.justification: Every element is the best semantic match: <button> for all interactive controls, <article> for product cards, <address> for contact info, <search> for the search bar, native <input type='checkbox'> for filters. No div/span used where a semantic element exists.

aria_discipline.justification: ARIA is used sparingly and nav labels are correctly applied without the word 'navigation'. However, 'Add to cart' buttons use aria-describedby (which adds description, not name) rather than aria-label or aria-labelledby, so all six buttons share the identical accessible name 'Add to cart' — the distinguishability requirement is not met.

heading_hierarchy.justification: Single h1, no skipped levels. However, product names are placed at h2 directly under the h1 with no intermediate section heading, whereas the expected pattern (h1 page → h2 sections → h3 product names) was not followed. The heading level is also hardcoded without any note about configurability.

landmark_structure.justification: All five major landmarks (<header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>, <footer>) are used correctly, <search> is used for the search bar, and skip links ('Skip to main content', 'Skip to search', 'Skip to filters') are the first focusable elements. Both <nav> elements are labelled without the word 'navigation', and <main> contains only the page-unique primary content.

list_semantics.justification: Navigation items, filter options, and product cards are all wrapped in <ul>, correctly communicating count to assistive technology. Footer nav links are also in a <ul>. No lists are misused for purely decorative grouping.

form_semantics.justification: Every input has a visible <label>, filter groups are wrapped in <fieldset>/<legend>, the 'In stock only' toggle is a native checkbox, and appropriate input types are used (type='search', type='checkbox'). No placeholder-as-label patterns present.

content_realism.justification: All six products have realistic brand names, model numbers, and prices (e.g., Nike Air Zoom Pegasus 41 at $129.99). Descriptions are specific and varied in length. Navigation labels, contact details, and footer links are all plausible real-world content.

expectations_met: <main> contains only the primary page content — site header, footer, and primary nav are outside it
Skip navigation links are the first focusable elements — 'Skip to content' and 'Skip to search' both present
Navigation uses <nav> with a label that does NOT include the word 'navigation'
Filter sidebar uses <form> with <fieldset>/<legend> for each filter group
Buttons are <button> elements, not <div> or <a> with click handlers
Price is not marked up as a heading
No ARIA attributes used where native HTML provides the same semantics
Filter checkboxes use proper <label> elements, not placeholders
The 'in stock only' toggle uses a native checkbox
expectations_missed: Heading hierarchy should flow h1 → h2 (sections) → h3 (product names); instead product names are placed at h2 with no intermediate section heading
Product card heading level is hardcoded to h2 with no documentation that it should be configurable based on context

'Add to cart' buttons do not have accessible names that distinguish which product — aria-describedby provides description only and does not change the accessible name, leaving all six buttons with the identical name 'Add to cart'
additional_observations: Three skip links ('Skip to main content', 'Skip to search', 'Skip to filters') exceed the minimum requirement and are well-targeted to this page's structure
<button type='submit'> inside <search> has no parent <form>, so the submit type is semantically inert — wrapping in <form> with an action would be more correct
Using aria-label on both the <aside> and the nested <form> is slightly redundant but not harmful
